1: The ark is placed inside the tent (ʾōhel) David pitched — not the Mosaic tabernacle (mišķān), which remains at Gibeon (v39). This creates the two-sanctuary situation unique to David's reign: the ark without the tabernacle. The offerings here — ʿōlōt (burnt offerings) and šᵉlāmîm (peace/fellowship offerings) — are both required for covenant-inaugural ceremonies (cf. Exod 24:5).
2: David blesses the people bᵉšēm YHWH — in the name of YHWH. The verb bārak (to bless) in the piel, used of a king blessing the people, recalls Solomon's blessing at the temple dedication (1 Kgs 8:55-56) and Moses blessing Israel (Deut 33:1). Only priests typically blessed the assembly (Num 6:22-27); David's priestly-kingly role here is significant for the Chronicler's portrait.
3: The distribution of food: kikkār leḥem (a loaf of bread), ešpār (a cut of meat — the term appears only here and 2 Sam 6:19, likely a specific cut or portion), and ʾăšîšāh (raisin cake, associated with festive celebration, cf. Song 2:5). The communal meal following a covenant ceremony signals the completion of the event and the people's full incorporation into the celebration.
4: David appoints Levites to minister (lᵉšārēt) before the ark — three functions: lᵉhazķîr (to invoke, lit. “to cause to remember” — possibly a type of petition-prayer), lᵉhôdôt (to give thanks — hiphil of yādāh), and lᵉhallēl (to praise — piel of hālal). These three liturgical acts frame the psalm that follows and define the theological purpose of the Davidic worship establishment.
5: Asaph as chief (hārōʾš) of the Levitical worship ministers — the same Asaph after whom twelve of the psalms are titled (Pss 50, 73-83). The assignment of Obed-edom (cf. ch13) to a musical role here rehabilitates a figure who had hosted the ark; his family became a permanent fixture in the Jerusalem temple establishment.
6: The two priests, Benaiah and Jahaziel, are assigned taʿăśe baḥăṣōṣᵉrôt — to sound the trumpets (ḥăṣôṣᵉrôt) continually. The ḥăṣōṣᵉrāh (silver trumpet) was a distinctly priestly instrument (Num 10:1-10), different from the shofar; its continuous sounding marked the ongoing liturgical presence of the ark. The dual assignees mirrors the two silver trumpets of Num 10:2.
7: Bᵉyôm hahûʾ bāz dāwîd lᵉrōʾš lᵉhôdôt laYHWH bᵉyad-ʾĀsāp wᵉʾeḥāyw — on that day David first appointed Asaph and his brothers. The word bāz (then, at that time) marks the occasion as the inaugural moment of the Davidic liturgical arrangement. The psalm that follows (vv8-36) is presented as the foundational act of worship — composed or assembled by David, delivered through Asaph.
8: hôdû laYHWH qirᵉʾû bišmô — Give thanks to the LORD, call on his name. This opening matches Ps 105:1 almost verbatim. hôdû is the hiphil imperative of yādāh, a verb whose root meaning includes “to throw/cast” and has developed the theological sense of acknowledging what is true about someone — here, YHWH's acts. hôdîʿû bāʿammîm ʿălîlôtāyw — make known his deeds among the peoples: the proclamation is explicitly universal in scope from the first verse.
9: šîrû-lô zammᵉrû-lô — sing to him, make music to him. The two imperatives pair singing (šîr) with instrumental/melodic performance (zāmar in the piel, associated with skilled musical playing). śîḥû — meditate on/tell of (śîaḥ, to talk about, reflect on) — his wondrous works (niplᵉʾōtāyw, the niphal participle = things made wonderful).
10: hithallᵉlû bᵉšēm qodsô — glory/boast in his holy name. The hithpael of hālal is reflexive boasting — the worshiper acknowledges that YHWH's name is the proper object of pride, redirecting the human impulse to self-glorification. yiśmah lēb mᵉḇaqᵉšê YHWH — let the heart of those who seek YHWH rejoice: the participle mᵉḇaqᵉšê (those seeking) is the continuous active seeking that characterizes the covenant community.
11: dirᵉšû YHWH wᵉʿuzzô — seek the LORD and his strength. Two near-synonyms: dāraš (to seek, inquire, require) and the following baqqᵉšû (seek, look for) — the pairing emphasizes active, persistent seeking. ʿuzzô (his strength) in context likely refers to the ark as the physical locus of divine power (cf. Ps 78:61, where the ark is called “his strength”). paḇqᵉšû pānāyw tāmîd — seek his presence (pānāyw = his face) continually.
12: niflᵉʾōtāyw ʾăšer ʿāśāh mōpᵉtāyw ûmišpᵉṭê-pîhû — his wondrous works, his miracles, and the judgments of his mouth. Three terms for divine acts: niplāʾôt (wonders, amazing acts), môpᵉtîm (signs, portents — typically miraculous signs confirming a prophetic word), and mišpᵉṭê pîhû (judgments of his mouth — spoken divine verdicts). The phrase surveys the full range of YHWH's self-revelation in history.
13: zeraʿ Yiśrāʾēl ʿaḇdô bᵉnê Yaʿăqōḇ bᵉḥîrāyw — offspring of Israel his servant, children of Jacob, his chosen ones. ʿeḇed (servant) — Israel is YHWH's ʿeḇed, a title of honor in covenantal relationship (cf. Abraham in Gen 26:24; David in v7 of this chapter; Isaiah's Servant Songs). bᵉḥîrāyw (his chosen ones) — from bāḥar, the election verb; Israel as the elect nation.
14: hûʾ YHWH ʾĕlōhênû bᵉḵol-hāʾāreṣ mišpāṭāyw — He is the LORD our God; his judgments are in all the earth. The first clause is a covenant identification formula (hûʾ ʾĕlōhênû = he is our God) that echoes the Shema structure. The second clause asserts universal scope: YHWH's governance (mišpāṭîm) is not limited to Israel but extends over all the earth.
15: zikrû lᵉʿôlām bᵉrîtô — remember his covenant forever. bᵉrît (covenant) is the key term of the entire psalm; the rest of the psalm (vv16-18) specifies its content. dāḇār ṣiwwāh lᵉʾelep dôr — the word commanded for a thousand generations: the number “thousand generations” is a hyperbole of permanence, echoing the Decalogue (Exod 20:6; Deut 7:9).
16: ʾăšer kārat ʾet-ʾAḇrāhām ûšᵉḇûʿātô lᵉYiṣḥāq — the covenant he cut (kārat, the standard idiom for making a covenant, lit. “to cut”) with Abraham and his sworn oath to Isaac. šᵉḇûʿāh (oath) is the self-binding promise by which a covenant is sealed — YHWH swears by himself (cf. Gen 22:16; Heb 6:13) since there is no greater authority by which to swear.
17: wayyaʿămîdehā lᵉYaʿăqōḇ lᵉḥōq lᵉYiśrāʾēl bᵉrît ʿôlām — he confirmed it to Jacob as a statute, to Israel as an everlasting covenant. hēqîm (hiphil of qûm) = to establish, make stand firm — the verb used throughout the OT for the confirmation and guarantee of a covenant promise. ḥōq (statute) here elevates the covenant promise to the status of law — something bound by divine decree, not merely verbal promise.
18: lᵉḵā ʾettēn ʾet-ʾereṣ kᵉnaʿan ḥeḇel naḥălatkhem — to you I will give the land of Canaan as your portion of inheritance. ḥeḇel (measuring line, portion) — the land is described as a measured allotment, a surveyor's portion. The Promised Land as naḥălāh (inheritance/patrimony) is the standard covenantal term for the land-grant; inheritance language implies permanent familial ownership, not temporary occupation.
19: bihyôtᵉkem mᵉtê mispar kimᵉʿaṭ wᵉgārîm bāh — when you were few in number, of little account, and strangers in it. The two terms: mᵉtê mispar (men of number = countable, i.e., few) and gārîm (sojourners/strangers — the gēr status, those without land rights). The contrast with Israel's later population and land ownership makes the faithfulness of YHWH more striking: the promise was given when Israel had nothing.
20: wayyithalᵉkû miggôy ʾel-gôy ûmimmamlaḵāh ʾel-ʿam ʾaḥēr — they wandered from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another people. The hithpael of hālaḵ (to walk) in the reflexive/iterative sense describes the patriarchal wandering — nomadic, displacement, vulnerability. The repeated miggôy ʾel-gôy (from nation to nation) emphasizes the contrast with YHWH's faithfulness across that wandering.
21: lōʾ-hinnîaḥ ʾādām lᵉʿošqām — he allowed no man to oppress them. ʿāšaq (to oppress, exploit, crush) — the term used for oppression of the weak in the covenant law texts (Lev 19:13; Deut 24:14). YHWH's protection during the patriarchal period is framed as active legal advocacy — he “rebuked kings on their account” (note the extraordinary claim that even Pharaoh was rebuked by YHWH before Moses's time, cf. Gen 12:17; 20:3).
22: ʾal-tiggᵉʿû bimšîḥāy wûḇinḇîʾay ʾal-tārēʿû — do not touch my anointed ones, and to my prophets do no harm. mᵉšîḥāy (my anointed ones) is plural — the patriarchs are the referents in context (Ps 105:15). The term māšîaḥ (anointed) at this stage applies to those whom YHWH has set apart for a special role; the singular messianic use develops later. nāḇîʾ (prophet) — the patriarchs as prophets is explicit in Gen 20:7 (Abraham).
23: šîrû laYHWH kol-hāʾāreṣ — sing to the LORD, all the earth! This verse begins the Psalm 96 section of the composite psalm (Ps 96:1-13). The shift from Ps 105's historical rehearsal to Ps 96's universal summons marks a climax: having recalled what YHWH has done for Israel, the psalm now calls all creation to respond. baśśᵉrû miyyôm-ʾel-yôm yᵉšûʿātô — proclaim his salvation day to day: baśar (to bring good news, announce — the root of bᵉśôrāh, good news/gospel).
24: sappᵉrû ḇagôyim kᵉḇôdô bᵉḵol-hāʿammîm niplᵉʾōtāyw — declare his glory among the nations, his marvelous works among all peoples. The Chronicler preserves the universalist vision of Ps 96 intact: YHWH's kāḇôd (glory, weighty presence) is for all nations, not Israel alone. This is the theological framework for the temple that follows in 1 Chr 28-29: Solomon's temple is to be a house of prayer for all peoples (cf. Isa 56:7).
25: kî gādôl YHWH ûmᵉhullāl mᵉʾōd wᵉnôrāʾ hûʾ ʿal-kol-ʾĕlōhîm — for great is the LORD and greatly to be praised; he is to be feared above all gods. mᵉhullāl — pual participle of hālal = worthy of praise, praiseworthy (the passive: one to whom praise is properly directed). nôrāʾ (feared, awesome) — the niphal participle of yārēʾ, describing YHWH's numinous quality that evokes reverent awe.
26: kî kol-ʾĕlōhê hāʿammîm ʾĕlîlîm wîhwāh šāmayim ʿāśāh — for all the gods of the peoples are idols (ʾĕlîlîm), but the LORD made the heavens. The word ʾĕlîlîm is a diminutive or pejorative derived from ʾēl (God), suggesting “worthless little gods” — the same root used in the prophets for images that are nothing (Isa 2:8, 18; 10:10-11). The contrast: the gods of the nations did not make the heavens; YHWH did.
27: hôd wᵉhādār lᵉpānāyw ʿōz wᵉḥedwāh bimqômô — splendor and majesty before him; strength and joy in his place/dwelling. The four terms form two pairs: hôd (majesty, a quality of royalty, cf. Ps 21:6) and hādār (splendor, honor, adornment); ʿōz (strength, power) and ḥedwāh (joy, gladness). The Chronicler substitutes ḥedwāh (joy) where Ps 96:6 has tipreret (glory/beauty) — a small but theologically significant change emphasizing that YHWH's presence is characterized by joy.
28: hāḇû laYHWH mišpᵉḥôt ʿammîm — ascribe to the LORD, O families of the peoples. The verb yāhab (imperative hāḇû) means “give, render, ascribe” — a term of attribution. mišpᵉḥôt ʿammîm (families/clans of the peoples) addresses all human kindreds, not just Israel — the universal scope of the summons is maintained throughout the Ps 96 section.
29: hāḇû laYHWH kᵉḇôd šᵉmô — ascribe to the LORD the glory due his name. kᵉḇôd šᵉmô (the glory of his name) — kāḇôd is both the inherent weighty glory that belongs to YHWH and the reputation/honor that humans acknowledge. wᵉhišταḥăwû laYHWH bᵉhadrat-qōdeš — worship the LORD in the splendor of holiness. hadrat-qōdeš (splendor/beauty of holiness) is ambiguous: it may mean worshiping in holy attire, or in the beauty that holiness brings, or in the holy precincts. The phrase emphasizes that authentic worship has an aesthetic dimension — it is beautiful because holiness is beautiful.
30: ḥîlû mippānāyw kol-hāʾāreṣ — tremble before him (ḥîl — to writhe, to be in travail, to tremble) all the earth. The verb ḥûl/ḥîl connotes the trembling associated with both fear and birth-pangs — a visceral response to divine presence. ʾap-tikkôn têḇēl bal-timmôṭ — the world is firmly established; it cannot be moved: the stability of creation is grounded in YHWH's kingship (cf. Ps 93:1).
31: yiśmᵉḥû haššāmayim wᵉtāgēl hāʾāreṣ — let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice. The jussive forms invite cosmic celebration. wᵉyōmᵉrû ḇagôyim YHWH mālaḵ — and let them say among the nations: “The LORD reigns!” (mālaḵ — perfect of mālaḵ, “to reign” — the acclamation formula for a newly enthroned king). The cry YHWH mālaḵ recurs throughout the enthronement psalms (Pss 93:1; 96:10; 97:1; 99:1) and signals the comprehensive sovereignty of YHWH over creation and history.
32: yirʿam hayyām ûmᵉlōʾô — let the sea roar and all it contains. The sea (yām) was associated in ancient Near Eastern cosmology with primordial chaos; its joyful roaring is an inversion — chaos is invited to join creation's praise. yaʿălōz haśśādeh wᵉḵol-ʾăšer-bô — let the field exult and all that is in it: agricultural abundance participates in eschatological praise.
33: ʾāz yᵉrannᵉnû ʿăṣê hayyaʿar mippᵉnê YHWH — then the trees of the forest will sing for joy before the LORD. The particle ʾāz (then, at that time) marks the eschatological moment: when YHWH comes to judge, creation breaks into song. kî-ḇāʾ lišpôṭ ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — for he comes to judge the earth: the coming of YHWH as judge is presented as good news that creation celebrates, not as a threat. Judgment (šāpaṭ) here means establishing righteous order, not merely punishing evil.
34: hôdû laYHWH kî-ṭôḇ kî lᵉʿôlām ḥasdô — give thanks to the LORD, for he is good; for his steadfast love endures forever. This refrain (from Ps 107:1; 118:1, 29; 136:1) is the most repeated single verse in the Hebrew psalter. ḥesed — the single most important word in this verse: variously translated “steadfast love,” “covenant loyalty,” “lovingkindness,” or “mercy.” It denotes the persistent, covenant-faithful love that refuses to abandon its object regardless of the object's failures — the love that is simultaneously personal affection and legal obligation. The MKT renders it “steadfast love” throughout.
35: ûmōšîʿēnû wᵉqabbᵉṣēnû min-haggôyim — save us and gather us from among the nations. The two requests use the hiphil of yāšaʿ (save) and the piel of qāḇaṣ (gather). The qāḇaṣ (gather) verb is the standard term for the eschatological regathering of exiled Israel (Isa 40:11; 43:5; Jer 31:10; Ezek 37:21). The presence of this petition in the psalm composed for the ark's installation is theologically striking: even at the moment of triumph, the psalm anticipates future exile and regathering.
36: bārûk YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl min-hāʿôlām wᵉʿad-hāʿôlām — Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, from everlasting to everlasting. This closing doxology (from Ps 106:48) marks the structural division between the third book and fourth book of the psalter. wāʾōmer kol-hāʿām ʾāmēn ûhallēl laYHWH — all the people said “Amen” and “Praise the LORD.” ʾāmēn (so be it, truly) is the congregation's ratification of the doxology; the Chronicler adds the people's response to what is a psalm-closing formula in the psalter.
37: David leaves Asaph before the ark lišrōt lipnê hāʾārôn tāmîd — to minister before the ark continually (tāmîd). The term tāmîd (continually, perpetually) is the same word used for the ʿōlat hatāmîd (the continual burnt offering, Num 28:3-8) — Asaph's ministry is framed as a liturgical constant parallel to the sacrificial system.
38: Obed-edom with 68 kin assigned as gatekeepers (šōʿărîm) — a role of honor at the ark's tent. The Obed-edom who hosted the ark for three months (ch13) is now permanently integrated into the ark-ministry staff. šōʿēr (gatekeeper) was a major Levitical role at the sanctuary, controlling access and maintaining the sanctity of the space.
39: Zadok the priest is stationed at the tabernacle (mišķan YHWH) at the high place of Gibeon — the Mosaic tabernacle without the ark. The split arrangement (Asaph with the ark in Jerusalem, Zadok at the tabernacle in Gibeon) is the Chronicler's explanation of why both sites were legitimate during David's reign: each had its proper component of the full worship apparatus.
40: lᵉhaʿălôt ʿōlôt laYHWH ʿal-mizbah hāʿōlāh tāmîd — to offer burnt offerings to the LORD on the altar of burnt offering continually. The ʿōlat hattāmîd (regular burnt offering) with its twice-daily offering (morning and evening, Num 28:3-8) is maintained at Gibeon. This establishes that the Mosaic sacrificial regulations are being honored even while the ark-worship proceeds separately in Jerusalem.
41: ûʿimmāhem Hêmān wîdûtûn wᵉše ʾār habᵉrûrîm ʾăšer niqqᵉḇû bišēmôt — with them were Heman and Jeduthun and the rest of those chosen and designated by name. bᵉrûrîm (chosen ones — from bārar, to select, purify, choose) suggests careful selection criteria; niqqᵉḇû bišēmôt (designated by name) means officially registered. The repeated precision of appointment underscores the Chronicler's theme: proper worship requires proper personnel, properly authorized.
42: Heman and Jeduthun had ḥăṣōṣᵉrôt ûmᵉṣillātayim (trumpets and cymbals) and kᵉlê šîr hāʾĕlōhîm — instruments of divine song (lit. “instruments of the song of God”). The possessive hāʾĕlōhîm (of God) applied to musical instruments is unusual and signals their sacred character. The sons of Jeduthun are assigned as gatekeepers, integrating the musical families into both liturgical and administrative roles.
43: wayyēlᵉkû kol-hāʿām ʾîš lᵉḇêtô — all the people went home, each to his house. The conclusion of the ark-installation ceremony marks the transition from the corporate event to domestic life. David's return to bless his household (lᵉḇārēk ʾet-bêtô) echoes the opening verse — the blessing that began with the ark's installation extends to the domestic sphere.
